refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined target. The latter is a success criterion dependent on operational execution, not a system requirement. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—providing visibility into throughput—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of whether a specific productivity target is met. The target itself belongs in acceptance criteria or KPI definitions, not in the FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports material throughput (m³/h or ton/h) during representative operations. Verify data accuracy against independent measurement. Confirm reporting is accessible to Site Manager and relevant stakeholders in real time or near-real time.
sub_frs: Acquire material throughput data (volume or mass per time interval) during active operations.
Calculate and aggregate throughput metrics in customer-specified units (m³/h or ton/h).
Report throughput metrics to authorized stakeholders with sufficient frequency and fidelity for operational decision-making.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput.
DP2: Measure discrete material batches (count, mass, or volume per batch) at regular intervals and extrapolate to throughput rate over evaluation period.
DP3: Monitor material level change in a bounded reservoir (input hopper or output stockpile) over time and infer net throughput from level differential and material density.
DP4: Combine multiple independent measurement modalities (e.g., batch counting + continuous flow sensing) with reconciliation logic to cross-validate throughput estimates.
DP5: Instrument material transport conveyance (belt speed + load mass, or pipe flow velocity + density) to compute throughput as a derived quantity from motion and material properties.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput.

potential_sources: Sensor selection (mass vs. volumetric) is decoupled from the logical DP; the choice depends on material type and operational context, not the measurement principle.
Integration method (numerical summation, time-weighted averaging) is independent of sensing modality.
Reporting format (units, frequency, dashboard vs. log file) is orthogonal to the measurement acquisition logic.
Calibration and drift correction are separate concerns that do not couple the core DP to specific sensor technologies.
coupling_check.notes: DP1 exhibits low coupling because the functional requirement (measure throughput) maps cleanly to a single logical solution (continuous flow acquisition + integration). Alternative DPs (DP2, DP3) introduce indirect inference chains (batch extrapolation, level change interpretation) that couple measurement to material properties and operational assumptions. DP4 and DP5 add redundancy and derived computation, increasing complexity without reducing fundamental uncertainty. DP1 is preferred for directness and independence from material-specific assumptions.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Continuous sensing hardware is available and can be integrated into the material stream without significant operational disruption. Risk: If worksite constraints prohibit in-stream instrumentation, DP1 may require redesign to non-contact or indirect sensing (e.g., DP3 or DP5).

Assumption: Material properties (density, particle size distribution) are stable or known during the evaluation period. Risk: Significant variation in material composition may degrade accuracy of volumetric-to-mass conversion or sensor calibration; mitigation requires periodic recalibration or dual-modality sensing (DP4).

Assumption: Sensor drift and systematic bias are negligible or can be corrected via pre- and post-evaluation calibration. Risk: If drift is significant during the evaluation period, reported throughput may not meet acceptance criteria; mitigation requires in-situ validation (e.g., DP4 cross-check).

Assumption: Integration period and sampling frequency are sufficient to capture representative throughput over the agreed evaluation window. Risk: If sampling is too coarse or evaluation period is too short, statistical confidence in throughput estimate may be insufficient; acceptance criteria must specify minimum evaluation duration and sampling rate.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan permits installation and operation of measurement instrumentation without creating new hazards (e.g., electrical safety, mechanical entanglement). Risk: If safety constraints are violated, instrumentation may be prohibited or require expensive mitigation (e.g., explosion-proof enclosures, guarding); early coordination with safety stakeholders is critical.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) Measurement modality preference (mass vs. volume) based on material type and operational context; (2) Required accuracy and confidence level (e.g., ±5% at 95% confidence); (3) Evaluation period duration and sampling frequency; (4) Worksite safety and operational constraints that may limit sensor placement or modality. With these clarifications, DP1 can be decomposed into sub-FRs for sensor selection (IM), signal conditioning (IM), data integration (IM), and reporting (IM), enabling detailed architectural design and ownership assignment.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized flow rate acquisition and throughput computation module responsible for continuous sensing integration, data aggregation, and cumulative metric calculation across the material handling system.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit instantaneous flow rate and cumulative throughput metrics to external monitoring/logging systems

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive evaluation period parameters and flow measurement configuration directives

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicate with distributed sensors on Vehicle or remote sensing infrastructure to acquire raw material stream property data (mass/volume signals)

module_containment_scores.notes: Strong containment: flow rate acquisition logic, integration algorithms, and cumulative throughput computation are core NGHE functions. Diffusion risk: raw sensor data originates on Vehicle; NGHE must interface via Network Link to retrieve instantaneous readings. Telemetry export is expected external coupling.

Weak containment: Vehicle hosts physical sensors but does not own flow computation logic. Integration and cumulative throughput calculation would diffuse to NGHE or Teleoperation. Sensor data acquisition could reside here, but DP emphasizes continuous sensing *and* integration—a split responsibility creates high coupling.

Poor containment: Teleoperation is a command/control interface, not a data acquisition or computation module. Flow rate sensing and integration are orthogonal to remote operation. High diffusion: logic would leak into NGHE Solution and Vehicle.

Poor containment: Safety Services is responsible for hazard detection and alert generation, not material flow measurement. Flow rate data may *feed* safety logic (e.g., overspeed detection), but acquisition and integration are not safety-specific concerns. High diffusion: core DP logic belongs elsewhere.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution is the logical owner of flow rate acquisition and throughput integration because it is the system-level intelligence layer responsible for material handling metrics, process monitoring, and data aggregation. Raw sensor signals originate on the Vehicle but are pulled/pushed via Network Link to NGHE for centralized processing. This avoids duplicating integration logic across multiple modules and maintains a single source of truth for cumulative throughput.
containment_risks: Sensor data latency: if Vehicle sensors are not continuously available or Network Link is intermittent, NGHE may not acquire true instantaneous flow rates; mitigation requires robust sensor-to-NGHE communication contract.

Sensor heterogeneity: if multiple Vehicle instances or sensor types exist, NGHE must normalize and reconcile disparate mass/volume signals; this could increase DP complexity if not anticipated in interface design.
Real-time constraint: if flow integration must occur at sub-second granularity, Network Link latency may violate timing requirements; consider edge processing on Vehicle as fallback.
Coupling to Telemetry: NGHE exports flow metrics via Telemetry; if Telemetry interface is slow or unreliable, throughput reporting may lag, creating a secondary diffusion risk.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP is well-formed and appropriately scoped. No revision needed. However, clarify the DP to explicitly state: 'Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) on the Vehicle, transmit raw signals to NGHE Solution via Network Link, and integrate over evaluation period in NGHE to compute cumulative throughput, exporting results via Telemetry.' This makes the sensor-to-computation boundary explicit and reduces ambiguity about where sensing vs. integration occurs.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 directly addresses FR by specifying a concrete mechanism: continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) integrated over an evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput. The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe specific sensor technology) and measurable against the acceptance criterion 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target over agreed evaluation period.' The logic is sound: instantaneous flow rate acquisition + temporal integration = cumulative throughput metric.

diffusion_risks: Sensor data latency: Network Link dependency introduces potential gaps in instantaneous flow rate acquisition if Vehicle sensors are intermittent or communication is delayed. Mitigation required: define sensor-to-NGHE communication SLA (e.g., max latency, availability threshold) to ensure integration fidelity.

Sensor heterogeneity diffusion: If multiple Vehicle instances or sensor types feed NGHE, normalization and reconciliation logic may scatter across Network Link interface handlers and NGHE core. Mitigation: centralize sensor abstraction layer within NGHE to normalize disparate mass/volume signals before integration.

Real-time integration constraint: If flow integration must occur at sub-second granularity (e.g., for safety-critical overspeed detection), Network Link latency may violate timing. Mitigation: clarify real-time requirement; if sub-second needed, consider edge processing on Vehicle with NGHE as aggregator.

Telemetry export coupling: NGHE exports cumulative throughput via Telemetry; if Telemetry is slow or unreliable, reported productivity may lag actual state, creating secondary diffusion of throughput computation logic into Teleoperation or Safety Services as workarounds. Mitigation: define Telemetry SLA and ensure NGHE maintains local throughput state independent of export success.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate owner (containment_score 0.85). Core DP logic—flow rate integration and cumulative throughput computation—is centralized in NGHE, avoiding duplication across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. Vehicle correctly scoped as sensor host (not computation owner); Teleoperation and Safety Services correctly excluded from flow measurement ownership. The 0.15 diffusion score reflects expected external coupling (sensor data ingress via Network Link, metric egress via Telemetry), which is architectural coupling, not logic diffusion. No unintended logic leakage detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Acceptance criterion 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target over agreed evaluation period' is directly verifiable against NGHE Solution's cumulative throughput output. Verification requires: (1) instrumented test with known material flow rate, (2) measurement of NGHE-reported cumulative throughput over evaluation period, (3) comparison to ground truth or productivity_target. Feasibility is high provided Network Link and Telemetry interfaces are operational during test. Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not impose additional constraints on measurement logic itself, though they may constrain test conditions.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns flow rate acquisition (via Network Link to Vehicle sensors) and integration logic; Vehicle owns sensor hardware; Telemetry owns metric export. Boundary between DP (continuous sensing + integration) and IM (NGHE module) is well-defined. However, the recommended revision below should be adopted to make sensor-to-computation boundary explicit in DP statement.

recommended_revisions: Revise DP1 to explicitly state the sensor-to-computation boundary: 'Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) on the Vehicle, transmit raw signals to NGHE Solution via Network Link, and integrate over evaluation period in NGHE to compute cumulative throughput, exporting results via Telemetry.' This removes ambiguity about where sensing vs. integration occurs and clarifies the DP-to-IM mapping.

Define Network Link SLA for sensor data: specify maximum latency, minimum availability, and data freshness requirements to ensure NGHE can acquire true instantaneous flow rates. Document fallback behavior if Network Link is intermittent.

Specify sensor normalization strategy in NGHE: if multiple Vehicle instances or sensor types exist, document how NGHE reconciles disparate mass/volume signals (e.g., unit conversion, outlier filtering, averaging) to prevent diffusion of normalization logic into interface handlers.

Clarify real-time integration constraint: if flow integration must occur at sub-second granularity (e.g., for safety-critical overspeed detection), document this requirement and assess whether Network Link latency is acceptable or edge processing on Vehicle is needed.

Define Telemetry SLA for throughput export: specify maximum latency and availability for cumulative throughput reporting to ensure reported productivity reflects actual state within acceptable bounds.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, indicating the decomposition is logically sound and architecturally feasible. However, the recommended_revisions from the gate analysis expose critical ambiguities in the DP statement that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-level decomposition. Specifically: (1) the DP conflates sensor acquisition (Vehicle responsibility) with integration (NGHE responsibility), creating unclear ownership boundaries; (2) Network Link and Telemetry SLAs are not specified, leaving diffusion risks unmitigated; (3) real-time integration constraints are not clarified, which is material given high safety criticality. Revising the DP and IM allocation now prevents downstream decomposition errors and ensures sub-FRs are derived from a clear, unambiguous parent requirement. The containment_score of 0.85 is acceptable but the 0.15 diffusion risk must be explicitly managed through SLA definition before sub-level work begins.

revise.why: DP1 must be split into two explicit sub-DPs to clarify the sensor-to-computation boundary: (1) DP1a: 'Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) on the Vehicle and transmit raw signals to NGHE Solution via Network Link with defined SLA (max latency, min availability, data freshness).' (2) DP1b: 'Integrate instantaneous flow rate signals received from Vehicle over evaluation period in NGHE Solution, normalize disparate sensor types/units, and compute cumulative throughput, exporting results via Telemetry with defined SLA (max latency, min availability).' This revision makes the DP-to-IM mapping explicit: Vehicle owns DP1a (sensor host), NGHE owns DP1b (computation and normalization), Network Link and Telemetry are interface mechanisms with contractual SLAs. Additionally, clarify whether real-time integration at sub-second granularity is required; if yes, assess whether Network Link latency is acceptable or edge processing on Vehicle is needed.

assumptions: Network Link is capable of delivering sensor data with latency and availability sufficient for NGHE to compute accurate cumulative throughput over the evaluation period.
Telemetry export mechanism is reliable and timely enough that reported productivity reflects actual NGHE state within acceptable bounds (not specified; requires SLA definition).
Vehicle sensors (mass or volume flow rate) are calibrated and provide instantaneous readings suitable for integration without additional preprocessing on Vehicle.
NGHE Solution has sufficient computational capacity and storage to maintain cumulative throughput state and handle multiple concurrent Vehicle sensor streams.
Sensor heterogeneity (multiple Vehicle types or sensor technologies) can be normalized within NGHE without requiring custom logic per Vehicle instance.
Real-time integration constraint (if any) is not sub-second; if sub-second is required, edge processing on Vehicle is acceptable.
open_risks: Network Link latency or intermittency could introduce gaps in instantaneous flow rate acquisition, degrading integration fidelity. Mitigation: define and validate Network Link SLA before NGHE deployment.

Sensor heterogeneity diffusion: if normalization logic scatters across Network Link interface handlers instead of centralizing in NGHE, maintenance and testing burden increases. Mitigation: enforce centralized sensor abstraction layer in NGHE design.

Telemetry export coupling: if Telemetry is slow or unreliable, reported productivity may lag actual state, creating pressure to duplicate throughput computation logic in Teleoperation or Safety Services. Mitigation: define Telemetry SLA and ensure NGHE maintains authoritative local state.

Real-time integration constraint not yet clarified: if safety-critical overspeed detection or other time-sensitive logic depends on sub-second flow integration, Network Link latency may be unacceptable. Mitigation: clarify real-time requirement and assess feasibility.

Verification test feasibility depends on Network Link and Telemetry being operational; if either is unavailable during test, acceptance criterion cannot be validated. Mitigation: include Network Link and Telemetry in test setup and define fallback verification approach.
evidence_needed: Network Link SLA specification: maximum latency (e.g., <100ms), minimum availability (e.g., 99.5%), data freshness requirement (e.g., no older than 1 second).
Telemetry SLA specification: maximum latency for cumulative throughput export, minimum availability, and fallback behavior if export fails.
Real-time integration requirement clarification: confirm whether sub-second integration is needed; if yes, assess Network Link latency acceptability or justify edge processing on Vehicle.
Sensor normalization strategy document: specify how NGHE reconciles disparate mass/volume signals, unit conversions, outlier filtering, and multi-Vehicle aggregation.
NGHE computational and storage capacity assessment: confirm NGHE can handle expected sensor data rate and maintain cumulative throughput state without loss or degradation.
Verification test plan: define instrumented test with known material flow rate, measurement protocol for NGHE-reported cumulative throughput, and comparison to ground truth or productivity_target.
Safety criticality impact assessment: confirm whether high safety criticality imposes additional real-time or fault-tolerance constraints on flow measurement logic.
